parts.text:
Oh, you want the real dirt? Alright, I’ll tell you about the time I single-handedly ruined a high-end corporate networking event because of my own overconfidence in my ability to multitask.

A few years ago, I was invited to this fancy "mixer" at a rooftop gallery. I’m talking $20 cocktails, people in tailored suits, and very expensive, very white minimalist art on the walls. I was trying desperately to look like I belonged there.

I was holding a glass of red wine in one hand and a plate of those tiny, greasy sliders in the other. I saw a potential client—the "Big Boss" of a firm I wanted to work with—standing near a floor-to-ceiling window. I decided this was my moment.

As I started walking over, I realized I had a massive glob of barbecue sauce on my thumb from the slider. I didn't have a napkin. Instead of doing the rational thing and going to the bathroom, I thought, *“I’ll just subtly wipe it on the bottom of my shoe as I walk. No one will see.”*

I tried to do this mid-stride. I lifted my foot slightly behind me to wipe my thumb on the heel. But I underestimated the friction of the polished gallery floor. 

My standing foot slipped. To compensate, I did this frantic, cartoonish leg-kick that looked like I was trying to perform a violent version of the Riverdance. 

In my panic, I squeezed the wine glass. The stem snapped. A literal arc of Cabernet Sauvignon flew through the air, perfectly tracking across the front of my light gray blazer and—I kid you not—landing directly on the shoulder of the Big Boss’s cream-colored cashmere coat.

The room went silent. I was standing there, panting, holding a broken glass shard, with barbecue sauce now smeared across my own forehead (don't ask me how, physics stopped working for a second). 

The Big Boss turned around, looked at the red stain spreading across his shoulder, looked at me, and then looked at the floor. I was so flustered that instead of an apology, the first thing that came out of my mouth was: 

"I was just trying to clean my thumb."

He just blinked at me, said "Mission accomplished," and walked away. I didn't even wait for my coat. I just walked straight out the door, down twelve flights of stairs because I couldn't face anyone in the elevator, and caught an Uber home while smelling like a fermented backyard grill. 

I still can't drink red wine without checking my surroundings for witnesses.
